title: Blood Cultures — Taking
oneLiner: A sterile, timed blood draw to detect bloodstream infection; proper technique prevents contamination, guides antibiotics, and saves lives in sepsis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
A 72-year-old man on the urology ward, post-TURP day 3, spikes a temperature of 39.2°C with rigors. His BP is 92/56, HR 110, and he looks flushed but confused. The nurse says he felt fine an hour ago. You note his urine bag is empty and he has a new suprapubic catheter. You suspect bacteremia from a urinary source.

- **Spiking fever + rigors** = classic bacteraemic ‘shower’; bacteria entering the blood triggers a cytokine cascade.  
- **Hypotension + tachycardia** = early distributive shock; endotoxin-mediated vasodilation.  
- **Confusion** = cerebral hypoperfusion or direct CNS effect of cytokines; red flag for sepsis.  
- **Recent urological instrumentation** = common portal for Gram-negative bacteraemia (E. coli, Klebsiella).  
- **Empty urine bag + new catheter** = possible retained urine infection seeding the blood.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Elderly with isolated new confusion, no fever** – hypothermia or relative bradycardia; always take cultures.  
- **Immunocompromised (neutropenic) patient** – may lack fever; look for rigors, hypotension, or simply “not right”.  
- **Post-operative surgical site infection** – wound may look benign; take cultures if there is any systemic upset.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Blood culture (BC) is a microbiological test in which blood is drawn into specialised bottles containing broth to grow aerobic and anaerobic bacteria (and sometimes fungi). The goal is to confirm or exclude bloodstream infection (bacteraemia/fungaemia) and identify the pathogen for targeted therapy.

- **Clinical stakes:** Sepsis kills ~11 million people/year globally; every hour delay in appropriate antibiotics increases mortality by ~7% (Kumar, 2006). Blood cultures are the gold standard for guiding therapy, detecting resistance, and shortening treatment duration.  
- **Common pitfall:** Contaminated cultures (e.g. from skin flora) lead to unnecessary antibiotics, longer hospital stays, and missed true diagnoses. False-negative cultures (e.g. due to prior antibiotics) delay life-saving treatment.  
- **Frequency:** Every junior doctor will take blood cultures multiple times a week; mastering the technique is a core competency.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
Bacteraemia occurs when bacteria invade the bloodstream from a focal infection (e.g. pneumonia, UTI, line, abscess). The immune response – mainly TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 from macrophages – triggers fever, rigors, vasodilation, and (in severe cases) multi-organ failure.

- **Why fever and rigors happen:** Bacterial cell wall components (LPS in Gram-negatives, lipoteichoic acid in Gram-positives) bind to Toll-like receptors on immune cells → cytokine release → hypothalamic prostaglandin E2 → fever. Rigors are involuntary muscle contractions to generate heat.
- **Why timing matters:** Bacteraemia is often intermittent (especially early in sepsis). Bacteraemic ‘spikes’ occur right before or during a fever. Taking cultures at the onset of rigors – before the fever peaks – maximises yield because bacterial clearance is slower than the temperature rise.
- **Why contamination happens:** Skin flora (e.g. *Staphylococcus epidermidis*, *Corynebacterium*) enter the bottle if aseptic technique fails. These grow in the bottle but are clinically irrelevant, wasting resources and risking overtreatment.
- **Why volume matters:** Bacteria density in blood is often low (≤10 CFU/mL). Adult bottles need 8–10 mL per bottle to reliably detect ≥1 CFU. Underfilling halves the sensitivity.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
Blood cultures are taken when there is clinical suspicion of bloodstream infection. The ‘causes’ are the sources of bacteraemia. Below are common categories with stem clues.

| Category                        | Most common organism(s)               | Stem clue                                            |
|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| **Community-acquired**          | *E. coli, S. pneumoniae, S. aureus*  | Acute onset fever + focal symptoms (cough, dysuria)   |
| **Hospital-acquired (nosocomial)** | *MRSA, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus*  | Fever after ≥48 h of admission, recent surgery/line |
| **Central line–associated** (CLABSI) | *Coag-neg staph, S. aureus, Candida* | Fever with no clear source; line site sometimes erythematous |
| **Endocarditis**                 | *Viridans group strep, S. aureus*     | Fever + new murmur + embolic phenomena (splinter haemorrhages) |
| **Immunocompromised/neutropenic** | *Pseudomonas, Candida, Aspergillus* | Fever >38.3°C with ANC <500; often no localising signs |
| **Intravenous drug use**         | *S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas*     | Fever + track marks + possible septic emboli          |
| **Unexplained fever** (Fever of Unknown Origin) | Various                     | Prolonged fever >3 weeks after initial workup – all negatives |

**Most common cause overall:** Community-acquired Gram-negative bacteraemia from a urinary source (*E. coli*) in elderly or catheterised patients.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
Blood culture is itself an investigation. The “investigation” here refers to the procedure and interpretation.

**Procedure (how to take correctly):**
1. **Identify need** – sepsis, fever >38.5°C, rigors, new murmur, line infection, or as per local policy (e.g. every febrile neutropenic patient).  
2. **Aseptic technique** – clean site with 2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol (or betadine if allergy) using concentric circles outward. Allow at least 30 seconds to dry (kills skin flora).  
3. **Venipuncture** – use a sterile needle and syringe. *Do not* palpate vein after cleaning. *Do not* draw through an existing line unless investigating line sepsis.  
4. **Fill bottles** – inoculate **aerobic bottle first** (prevents air entry into anaerobic), then **anaerobic bottle**. Each bottle needs 8–10 mL in adults (paediatric bottles ~1–3 mL). Minimum 5 mL per bottle for reasonable yield.  
5. **Two sets from two separate sites** – e.g. left antecubital and right antecubital. Must be drawn at different times unless the patient is on antibiotics (then draw two sets 30 min apart).  
6. **Label immediately** – patient details, date, time, site, and which bottle is aerobic/anaerobic.  
7. **Transport to lab within 2 hours** – or keep at room temperature (do not refrigerate).

**Interpretation:**

| Finding                                    | What it means                                                                 |
|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Positive (growth in ≥1 bottle)**         | True bacteraemia if same organism in ≥2 sets; contaminant if only 1 set and slow growth |
| **Time to positivity (TTP)**               | TTP <12 h → high bacterial load → likely true infection; >24 h → consider contaminant |
| **Common contaminants**                    | *Coagulase-negative staphylococci* (1 set only), *Corynebacterium*, *Bacillus*, *Micrococcus* |
| **Polymicrobial growth**                   | Risk of translocation from gut or multiple infected sites; think intra-abdominal source |
| **Negative after 5 days (no growth)**      | Active infection unlikely; consider non-infectious SIRS, viral, or fastidious organisms (e.g. *Bartonella*, *Legionella*) |
| **Single positive bottle with S. aureus**  | Always treat as true bacteraemia until proven otherwise – high pathogenicity |

**Single finding that confirms true infection:** >1 bottle growing the same organism (especially if drawn from separate sites at different times) → ≥95% positive predictive value.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management is about what you do *after* taking blood cultures – interpreting results and treating. But also, the decision **when** to take them.

**Immediate (within first hour):**
- **Take blood cultures BEFORE first dose of antibiotics** – but only if it does not delay antibiotics >45 minutes in septic shock. In stable patients, wait 15 minutes to draw before starting.  
- **Why:** Antibiotics rapidly reduce bacterial load in blood; even one dose can render cultures negative (false negative).  
- **If unable to delay (e.g. unstable septic shock):** Start empiric antibiotics immediately, then draw cultures as soon as possible.  

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Review preliminary gram stain result (usually 12–24 h) and full identification (24–48 h).**  
- **Adjust empiric therapy** to targeted antibiotics based on sensitivities.  
- **Common empiric regimens (doses):**  
  - **Community-acquired sepsis**: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (or ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h) + gentamicin 5–7 mg/kg IV q24h if severe.  
  - **Hospital-acquired/line-related**: Vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV q12h (trough 15–20) + piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (or meropenem 1g IV q8h if highly resistant).  
  - **Neutropenic sepsis**: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (or meropenem if penicillin allergy) + gentamicin once daily.  
- **If contaminant suspected (single bottle, slow growth, skin flora):** No antibiotics; repeat culture if clinical suspicion remains. Document as contaminant.

**Long-term (>72 hours):**
- **Repeat blood cultures if fever persists >48 h** on appropriate therapy – suggests inadequate source control, resistant organism, or secondary infection.  
- **For S. aureus bacteraemia:** Always order echocardiogram (TTE ± TOE) to rule out endocarditis. Minimum treatment duration 14 days (uncomplicated) or 4–6 weeks (complicated).  
- **For Candida in blood:** Remove all central lines if possible, start echinocandin (e.g. caspofungin 70mg IV load then 50mg IV daily).  
- **Pitfall to avoid: DON'T** start antibiotics blindly without cultures in a stable patient with fever – you’ll never know the true pathogen, leading to prolonged broad-spectrum therapy and resistance.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
When you get a positive blood culture, you must decide if it represents true infection or contamination. The main differential is between true bacteraemia and contaminated cultures, but also between different sources of bacteraemia. Below compares the presentation of a true Gram-negative rod (e.g. *E. coli*) vs contaminated *Coagulase-negative staph* vs viral sepsis (flu) vs non-infectious SIRS (e.g. pancreatitis).

| Feature              | True bacteraemia (Gram-neg) | Contaminated culture (CoNS) | Viral sepsis (e.g. influenza) | Non-infectious SIRS (pancreatitis) |
|----------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Fever pattern**     | High, spiking + rigors      | Low-grade or absent         | Moderate, often prodrome      | Variable; often absent             |
| **Number of positive bottles** | ≥2 sets, both same organism | 1 set (often slow growth)   | Negative cultures              | Negative cultures                  |
| **Time to positivity**| <12 hours                   | >24 hours                   | N/A                           | N/A                                |
| **Clinical focus**    | UTI, pneumonia, line, etc.  | No clear source             | Respiratory symptoms, myalgia | Epigastric pain, raised amylase    |
| **Lactate**           | Often elevated (>2)         | Normal                      | May be normal or mild         | Can be elevated (hypoperfusion)    |
| **WCC**               | Elevated or low (neutropenic) | Normal                     | Low (lymphopenia)             | Elevated (acute phase)             |
| **Key differentiator**| >1 positive bottle + same organism + clinical focus | Single bottle with skin flora in absence of other evidence | Negative cultures + viral PCR positive | Negative cultures + imaging findings |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Missed true bacteraemia (i.e. not taking cultures, or taking incorrectly):**
- **Sepsis → septic shock → multi-organ failure** – progression from SIRS to DIC, ARDS, AKI.  
- **Metastatic infection** – *S. aureus* can seed bones (osteomyelitis), heart valves (endocarditis), joints, and organs (splenic abscess).  
- **Candidemia** – if untreated, mortality >60%; often seeds retina, kidneys, and brain.  
- **Endocarditis** – especially in IV drug users or those with prosthetic valves; presents with persistent fever, new murmur, embolic events (splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions, Roth spots).  

**Late-presentation patterns (what to look for in a deteriorating patient who was not cultured):**
- Persistent fever despite empiric antibiotics – suspect resistant organism or undrained abscess.  
- New heart murmur + splinter haemorrhages → suspect endocarditis from unaddressed bacteraemia.  
- Unexplained hypotension after initial resuscitation – look for source, repeat lactate, consider fungal sepsis.  

**Contaminated cultures (false positive):**  
- Leads to unnecessary antibiotics (e.g. vancomycin) → renal toxicity, C. diff colitis.  
- Extended hospital stay for “rule out sepsis”.  
- Formal labs and ID consults that waste resources.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **FILL FIRST** – draw blood cultures **F**irst, **I**nvestigate source, **L**ine check, **L**actate, then **F**luids, **I**ncrease O₂, **R**epeat vitals, **S**tart antibiotics, **T**ransfer if needed. (Acronym for sepsis 6, but emphasises cultures first.)
- **2 x 2 x 2** – **2** sets from **2** sites, **2** minutes apart (if not on antibiotics). Each bottle needs **2** mL (paediatric) or **8**–**10** mL (adult).

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“Rigors = culture now.”** The presence of rigors (teeth-chattering, uncontrollable shaking) predicts bacteraemia with high specificity (~80%). Do not wait for the fever to peak.
2. **“One bottle for one organism = contaminant until proven otherwise.”** If a single bottle grows *Coagulase-negative staph*, and the patient looks well, do *not* start antibiotics. Repeat culture.
3. **“In sepsis, every hour counts, but 15 minutes to draw cultures is usually safe.”** In stable septic patients (lactate <4, BP stable), wait 15 min for cultures before abx. In septic shock (MAP <65, lactate >4), start abx immediately.
4. **“The bottle is sterile – the skin is not.”** Asepsis is *everything*. Use patient contact to clean, allow to dry, and never touch the vein after cleaning.
5. **“Volume is the most important determinant of positivity.”** A 10 mL bottle with only 3 mL blood loses >50% sensitivity. Train yourself to feel the blood flow – a full 10 mL is more than you think.
6. **“Line cultures? Only if you suspect line infection.”** If you draw from a line, you must draw a peripheral set simultaneously. A positive line culture alone is difficult to interpret.
7. **“Time to positivity (TTP) <12 hours is dangerous.”** Very fast growth (e.g. <6 h) suggests high bacterial load → think Gram-negative sepsis or severe *S. aureus* infection – escalate.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- **Drawing from the same vein after antibiotic administration** – wait at least 30 min after IV antibiotics to avoid false negatives.
- **Using alcohol alone without chlorhexidine** – alcohol alone does not kill *Bacillus* spores.
- **Puncturing a haematoma** – increases contamination risk and is painful.
- **Forgetting to note which bottle is aerobic vs anaerobic** – leads to wasted lab time.
- **Culturing a line without informing the lab** – many labs discard line cultures if not specifically indicated.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
When to escalate care because blood culture results or patient condition demands urgent attention.

- **NEWS2 ≥5** or any single red score (e.g. systolic BP <90, resp rate >24) – activate sepsis 6 and inform registrar.  
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – once positive cultures pending, escalate to ICU/high dependency; consider central line and vasopressors.  
- **Positive Gram stain showing Gram-negative rods in an unstable patient** – emergently broaden antibiotics (e.g. meropenem) and discuss with microbiology.  
- **Positive blood culture in a patient with prosthetic valve or implant** – must have early echo and ID consult; treat as endocarditis until proven otherwise.  
- **Any positive culture in a neutropenic patient (ANC <500)** – immediate ID consult; regimen may need addition of gentamicin or antifungal.  
- **Resistant organism isolated (MRSA, VRE, ESBL, Candida)** – isolate patient, review antibiotics, and call infection control.  
- **Persistent positive cultures after 48 h of appropriate therapy** – suspect endocarditis, central line infection, or undrained abscess – call senior and microbiology.

**Crash team call:** If patient becomes hypotensive despite fluids, GCS <13, or has new arrhythmia with shock – call 2222, start CPR if needed. Blood cultures are secondary to ABC at that point.

## Blood Cultures — Taking

**One-liner:** A sterile, timed blood draw to detect bloodstream infection; proper technique prevents contamination, guides antibiotics, and saves lives in sepsis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

A 72-year-old man on the urology ward, post-TURP day 3, spikes a temperature of 39.2°C with rigors. His BP is 92/56, HR 110, and he looks flushed but confused. The nurse says he felt fine an hour ago. You note his urine bag is empty and he has a new suprapubic catheter. You suspect bacteremia from a urinary source.

- **Spiking fever + rigors** = classic bacteraemic ‘shower’; bacteria entering the blood triggers a cytokine cascade.  
- **Hypotension + tachycardia** = early distributive shock; endotoxin-mediated vasodilation.  
- **Confusion** = cerebral hypoperfusion or direct CNS effect of cytokines; red flag for sepsis.  
- **Recent urological instrumentation** = common portal for Gram-negative bacteraemia (E. coli, Klebsiella).  
- **Empty urine bag + new catheter** = possible retained urine infection seeding the blood.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Elderly with isolated new confusion, no fever** – hypothermia or relative bradycardia; always take cultures.  
- **Immunocompromised (neutropenic) patient** – may lack fever; look for rigors, hypotension, or simply “not right”.  
- **Post-operative surgical site infection** – wound may look benign; take cultures if there is any systemic upset.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Blood culture (BC) is a microbiological test in which blood is drawn into specialised bottles containing broth to grow aerobic and anaerobic bacteria (and sometimes fungi). The goal is to confirm or exclude bloodstream infection (bacteraemia/fungaemia) and identify the pathogen for targeted therapy.

- **Clinical stakes:** Sepsis kills ~11 million people/year globally; every hour delay in appropriate antibiotics increases mortality by ~7% (Kumar, 2006). Blood cultures are the gold standard for guiding therapy, detecting resistance, and shortening treatment duration.  
- **Common pitfall:** Contaminated cultures (e.g. from skin flora) lead to unnecessary antibiotics, longer hospital stays, and missed true diagnoses. False-negative cultures (e.g. due to prior antibiotics) delay life-saving treatment.  
- **Frequency:** Every junior doctor will take blood cultures multiple times a week; mastering the technique is a core competency.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

Bacteraemia occurs when bacteria invade the bloodstream from a focal infection (e.g. pneumonia, UTI, line, abscess). The immune response – mainly TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 from macrophages – triggers fever, rigors, vasodilation, and (in severe cases) multi-organ failure.

- **Why fever and rigors happen:** Bacterial cell wall components (LPS in Gram-negatives, lipoteichoic acid in Gram-positives) bind to Toll-like receptors on immune cells → cytokine release → hypothalamic prostaglandin E2 → fever. Rigors are involuntary muscle contractions to generate heat.
- **Why timing matters:** Bacteraemia is often intermittent (especially early in sepsis). Bacteraemic ‘spikes’ occur right before or during a fever. Taking cultures at the onset of rigors – before the fever peaks – maximises yield because bacterial clearance is slower than the temperature rise.
- **Why contamination happens:** Skin flora (e.g. *Staphylococcus epidermidis*, *Corynebacterium*) enter the bottle if aseptic technique fails. These grow in the bottle but are clinically irrelevant, wasting resources and risking overtreatment.
- **Why volume matters:** Bacteria density in blood is often low (≤10 CFU/mL). Adult bottles need 8–10 mL per bottle to reliably detect ≥1 CFU. Underfilling halves the sensitivity.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

Blood cultures are taken when there is clinical suspicion of bloodstream infection. The ‘causes’ are the sources of bacteraemia. Below are common categories with stem clues.

| Category                        | Most common organism(s)               | Stem clue                                            |
|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| **Community-acquired**          | *E. coli, S. pneumoniae, S. aureus*  | Acute onset fever + focal symptoms (cough, dysuria)   |
| **Hospital-acquired (nosocomial)** | *MRSA, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus*  | Fever after ≥48 h of admission, recent surgery/line |
| **Central line–associated** (CLABSI) | *Coag-neg staph, S. aureus, Candida* | Fever with no clear source; line site sometimes erythematous |
| **Endocarditis**                 | *Viridans group strep, S. aureus*     | Fever + new murmur + embolic phenomena (splinter haemorrhages) |
| **Immunocompromised/neutropenic** | *Pseudomonas, Candida, Aspergillus* | Fever >38.3°C with ANC <500; often no localising signs |
| **Intravenous drug use**         | *S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas*     | Fever + track marks + possible septic emboli          |
| **Unexplained fever** (Fever of Unknown Origin) | Various                     | Prolonged fever >3 weeks after initial workup – all negatives |

**Most common cause overall:** Community-acquired Gram-negative bacteraemia from a urinary source (*E. coli*) in elderly or catheterised patients.  

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

Blood culture is itself an investigation. The “investigation” here refers to the procedure and interpretation.

**Procedure (how to take correctly):**
1. **Identify need** – sepsis, fever >38.5°C, rigors, new murmur, line infection, or as per local policy (e.g. every febrile neutropenic patient).  
2. **Aseptic technique** – clean site with 2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol (or betadine if allergy) using concentric circles outward. Allow at least 30 seconds to dry (kills skin flora).  
3. **Venipuncture** – use a sterile needle and syringe. *Do not* palpate vein after cleaning. *Do not* draw through an existing line unless investigating line sepsis.  
4. **Fill bottles** – inoculate **aerobic bottle first** (prevents air entry into anaerobic), then **anaerobic bottle**. Each bottle needs 8–10 mL in adults (paediatric bottles ~1–3 mL). Minimum 5 mL per bottle for reasonable yield.  
5. **Two sets from two separate sites** – e.g. left antecubital and right antecubital. Must be drawn at different times unless the patient is on antibiotics (then draw two sets 30 min apart).  
6. **Label immediately** – patient details, date, time, site, and which bottle is aerobic/anaerobic.  
7. **Transport to lab within 2 hours** – or keep at room temperature (do not refrigerate).

**Interpretation:**

| Finding                                    | What it means                                                                 |
|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Positive (growth in ≥1 bottle)**         | True bacteraemia if same organism in ≥2 sets; contaminant if only 1 set and slow growth |
| **Time to positivity (TTP)**               | TTP <12 h → high bacterial load → likely true infection; >24 h → consider contaminant |
| **Common contaminants**                    | *Coagulase-negative staphylococci* (1 set only), *Corynebacterium*, *Bacillus*, *Micrococcus* |
| **Polymicrobial growth**                   | Risk of translocation from gut or multiple infected sites; think intra-abdominal source |
| **Negative after 5 days (no growth)**      | Active infection unlikely; consider non-infectious SIRS, viral, or fastidious organisms (e.g. *Bartonella*, *Legionella*) |

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management is about what you do *after* taking blood cultures – interpreting results and treating. But also, the decision **when** to take them.

**Immediate (within first hour):**
- **Take blood cultures BEFORE first dose of antibiotics** – but only if it does not delay antibiotics >45 minutes in septic shock. In stable patients, wait 15 minutes to draw before starting.  
- **Why:** Antibiotics rapidly reduce bacterial load in blood; even one dose can render cultures negative (false negative).  
- **If unable to delay (e.g. unstable septic shock):** Start empiric antibiotics immediately, then draw cultures as soon as possible.  

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Review preliminary gram stain result (usually 12–24 h) and full identification (24–48 h).**  
- **Adjust empiric therapy** to targeted antibiotics based on sensitivities.  
- **Common empiric regimens (doses):**  
  - **Community-acquired sepsis**: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (or ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h) + gentamicin 5–7 mg/kg IV q24h if severe.  
  - **Hospital-acquired/line-related**: Vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV q12h (trough 15–20) + piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (or meropenem 1g IV q8h if highly resistant).  
  - **Neutropenic sepsis**: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (or meropenem if penicillin allergy) + gentamicin once daily.  
- **If contaminant suspected (single bottle, slow growth, skin flora):** No antibiotics; repeat culture if clinical suspicion remains. Document as contaminant.

**Long-term (>72 hours):**
- **Repeat blood cultures if fever persists >48 h** on appropriate therapy – suggests inadequate source control, resistant organism, or secondary infection.  
- **For S. aureus bacteraemia:** Always order echocardiogram (TTE ± TOE) to rule out endocarditis. Minimum treatment duration 14 days (uncomplicated) or 4–6 weeks (complicated).  
- **For Candida in blood:** Remove all central lines if possible, start echinocandin (e.g. caspofungin 70mg IV load then 50mg IV daily).  
- **Pitfall to avoid: DON'T** start antibiotics blindly without cultures in a stable patient with fever – you’ll never know the true pathogen, leading to prolonged broad-spectrum therapy and resistance.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

When you get a positive blood culture, you must decide if it represents true infection or contamination. The main differential is between true bacteraemia and contaminated cultures, but also between different sources of bacteraemia. Below compares the presentation of a true Gram-negative rod (e.g. *E. coli*) vs contaminated *Coagulase-negative staph* vs viral sepsis (flu) vs non-infectious SIRS (e.g. pancreatitis).

| Feature              | True bacteraemia (Gram-neg) | Contaminated culture (CoNS) | Viral sepsis (e.g. influenza) | Non-infectious SIRS (pancreatitis) |
|----------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Fever pattern**     | High, spiking + rigors      | Low-grade or absent         | Moderate, often prodrome      | Variable; often absent             |
| **Number of positive bottles** | ≥2 sets, both same organism | 1 set (often slow growth)   | Negative cultures              | Negative cultures                  |
| **Time to positivity**| <12 hours                   | >24 hours                   | N/A                           | N/A                                |
| **Clinical focus**    | UTI, pneumonia, line, etc.  | No clear source             | Respiratory symptoms, myalgia | Epigastric pain, raised amylase    |
| **Lactate**           | Often elevated (>2)         | Normal                      | May be normal or mild         | Can be elevated (hypoperfusion)    |
| **WCC**               | Elevated or low (neutropenic) | Normal                     | Low (lymphopenia)             | Elevated (acute phase)             |
| **Key differentiator**| >1 positive bottle + same organism + clinical focus | Single bottle with skin flora in absence of other evidence | Negative cultures + viral PCR positive | Negative cultures + imaging findings |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Missed true bacteraemia (i.e. not taking cultures, or taking incorrectly):**
- **Sepsis → septic shock → multi-organ failure** – progression from SIRS to DIC, ARDS, AKI.  
- **Metastatic infection** – *S. aureus* can seed bones (osteomyelitis), heart valves (endocarditis), joints, and organs (splenic abscess).

- Persistent fever despite empiric antibiotics – suspect resistant organism or undrained abscess.  
- New heart murmur + splinter haemorrhages → suspect endocarditis from unaddressed bacteraemia.  
- Unexplained hypotension after initial resuscitation – look for source, repeat lactate, consider fungal sepsis.  

**Contaminated cultures (false positive):**  
- Leads to unnecessary antibiotics (e.g. vancomycin) → renal toxicity, C. diff colitis.  
- Extended hospital stay for “rule out sepsis”.  
- Formal labs and ID consults that waste resources.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **FILL FIRST** – draw blood cultures **F**irst, **I**nvestigate source, **L**ine check, **L**actate, then **F**luids, **I**ncrease O₂, **R**epeat vitals, **S**tart antibiotics, **T**ransfer if needed. (Acronym for sepsis 6, but emphasises cultures first.)
- **2 x 2 x 2** – **2** sets from **2** sites, **2** minutes apart (if not on antibiotics). Each bottle needs **2** mL (paediatric) or **8**–**10** mL (adult).

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“Rigors = culture now.”** The presence of rigors (teeth-chattering, uncontrollable shaking) predicts bacteraemia with high specificity (~80%). Do not wait for the fever to peak.
2. **“One bottle for one organism = contaminant until proven otherwise.”** If a single bottle grows *Coagulase-negative staph*, and the patient looks well, do *not* start antibiotics. Repeat culture.
3. **“In sepsis, every hour counts, but 15 minutes to draw cultures is usually safe.”** In stable septic patients (lactate <4, BP stable), wait 15 min for cultures before abx. In septic shock (MAP <65, lactate >4), start abx immediately.
4. **“The bottle is sterile – the skin is not.”** Asepsis is *everything*. Use patient contact to clean, allow to dry, and never touch the vein after cleaning.
5. **“Volume is the most important determinant of positivity.”** A 10 mL bottle with only 3 mL blood loses >50% sensitivity. Train yourself to feel the blood flow – a full 10 mL is more than you think.
6. **“Line cultures? Only if you suspect line infection.”** If you draw from a line, you must draw a peripheral set simultaneously. A positive line culture alone is difficult to interpret.
7. **“Time to positivity (TTP) <12 hours is dangerous.”** Very fast growth (e.g. <6 h) suggests high bacterial load → think Gram-negative sepsis or severe *S. aureus* infection – escalate.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- **Drawing from the same vein after antibiotic administration** – wait at least 30 min after IV antibiotics to avoid false negatives.
- **Using alcohol alone without chlorhexidine** – alcohol alone does not kill *Bacillus* spores.
- **Puncturing a haematoma** – increases contamination risk and is painful.
- **Forgetting to note which bottle is aerobic vs anaerobic** – leads to wasted lab time.
- **Culturing a line without informing the lab** – many labs discard line cultures if not specifically indicated.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

When to escalate care because blood culture results or patient condition demands urgent attention.

- **NEWS2 ≥5** or any single red score (e.g. systolic BP <90, resp rate >24) – activate sepsis 6 and inform registrar.  
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – once positive cultures pending, escalate to ICU/high dependency; consider central line and vasopressors.  
- **Positive Gram stain showing Gram-negative rods in an unstable patient** – emergently broaden antibiotics (e.g. meropenem) and discuss with microbiology.  
- **Positive blood culture in a patient with prosthetic valve or implant** – must have early echo and ID consult; treat as endocarditis until proven otherwise.  
- **Any positive culture in a neutropenic patient (ANC <500)** – immediate ID consult; regimen may need addition of gentamicin or antifungal.  
- **Resistant organism isolated (MRSA, VRE, ESBL, Candida)** – isolate patient, review antibiotics, and call infection control.  
- **Persistent positive cultures after 48 h of appropriate therapy** – suspect endocarditis, central line infection, or undrained abscess – call senior and microbiology.

**Crash team call:** If patient becomes hypotensive despite fluids, GCS <13, or has new arrhythmia with shock – call 2222, start CPR if needed. Blood cultures are secondary to ABC at that point.
